title: Joint Optimization of Qubit Leasing and Quantum Circuit Distribution
abstract: We consider an agent, who would like to execute a given quantum circuit using resources leased from a set of quantum computers (QCs) connected by a quantum network. For this purpose, the agent needs to make the following four key decisions: (i) how many qubits to lease from each QC, (ii) at which QCs to store different circuit qubits in different time slots, (iii) at which QC to execute each gate in the circuit, and (iv) how to move qubits between QCs, choosing between migration and teleportation. We refer to this problem facing the agent as the joint qubit leasing and quantum circuit distribution (JQLQCD) problem, and provide a comprehensive integer linear programming (ILP) formulation for it. We show that the JQLQCD problem is NP-complete. Next, we identify several special cases in which the problem can be optimally solved in closed form or via polynomial-time algorithms. Also, we propose a greedy algorithm with local search refinement to solve large instances of the general JQLQCD problem. Finally, we evaluate the performance of the proposed greedy algorithm using extensive numerical computations.
summary: This paper tackles the complex JQLQCD problem of scheduling quantum circuits across multiple quantum computers by jointly optimizing qubit leasing, storage, gate execution, and qubit movement. After proving the problem is NP-complete, the authors introduce a fast and effective greedy heuristic that achieves near-optimal results, making large-scale quantum circuit deployment more computationally feasible.
main_problem: The paper addresses the Joint Qubit Leasing and Quantum Circuit Distribution (JQLQCD) problem, which involves optimally scheduling and allocating qubits across multiple quantum computers connected by a network.
main_result: The problem is proven to be NP-complete, but the authors propose a highly efficient greedy heuristic that performs comparably to simulated annealing while being significantly faster.
method: The core methodology involves formulating the problem as an Integer Linear Program (ILP) and then developing and analyzing a greedy algorithm with local search refinement to solve large instances.
model_or_system: The model simulates a quantum circuit executed by an agent across a set of Quantum Computers (QCs) connected by a quantum network. The system must jointly manage qubit leasing, storage location, gate execution assignment, and qubit movement (migration vs. teleportation).
key_observables: Total system cost (minimization objective), circuit makespan ($T_{max}$), and computational execution time.
important_parameters: Leasing costs ($c_{s,p}, c_{e,p}$), gate execution costs ($c_{g,p}$), communication costs (migration $d$, teleportation $E$), and the weight $ eta$ assigned to the makespan.
main_assumptions: The model assumes passive QCs providing resources to a single agent, and the complexity analysis relies on bounding the circuit tree-width ($w_t$) for fixed-parameter tractability.
figures_summary: Figures compare the total cost and execution time of Optimal, Greedy, and Simulated Annealing algorithms across varying circuit sizes, number of QCs, and makespan weights, demonstrating the greedy algorithm's superior speed.
paper_structure: The paper reviews related work, presents the comprehensive ILP formulation, proves NP-completeness via reduction from $P|	ext{prec}|C_{max}$, analyzes solvable special cases, proposes the greedy heuristic, and concludes with extensive numerical performance evaluations.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial, computationally tractable framework for optimizing resource utilization in large-scale, distributed quantum computing architectures, which is a major bottleneck for near-term quantum applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. The figure illustrates the system model. The agent leases resources from multiple QCs (QC p1 to QC p|P |) connected by a quantum network. Each QC p has qubit storage capacity sp and qubit execution capacity ep, and charges costs cs p and ce p for leasing storage and execution qubits, respectively. The execution cost of gate g at QC p is cg p. The figure also shows the quantum circuit that the agent would like to run using the leased resources.
Fig. 2. The figure shows the total cost versus number of gates for the greedy algorithm and SA.

Fig. 3 compares the execution times for the same problem instances as for Fig. 2. The greedy algorithm is consistently 50 −200× faster than SA. The greedy algorithm’s execution time grows approximately as O(|G|1.2), close to the theoretical O(|G|) bound, while SA’s execution time grows super-linearly at approximately O(|G|1.8). For the largest instances (|G| = 200), the greedy algorithm completes in less than 1 second, while SA requires over 100 seconds.
Fig. 3. The figure shows the execution time versus number of gates for the greedy algorithm and SA.
Fig. 4. The figure shows the total cost versus number of QCs for the greedy algorithm and SA.
